en.extract:
The Cape robin-chat (Dessonornis caffer) is a small passerine bird of the Old World flycatcher family Muscicapidae. It has a disjunct range from South Sudan to South Africa.
The locally familiar and confiding species has colonized and benefited from a range of man-altered habitats, including city suburbs and farmstead woodlots. It is an accomplished songster like other robin-chats, but is rather less colourful than most, and frequents either drier settings or higher altitudes. It forages in the proximity of cover, in the open or in fairly well-lit environments. Its distribution resembles that of the karoo–olive complex of thrushes, but it prefers the bracken-briar fringes of Afromontane forest, and does not enter far into forest proper.  It is altitudinally segregated from the red-capped robin-chat, and is less of a skulker.


Taxonomy
The Cape robin-chat was formally described in 1771 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus under the binomial name Motacilla caffra. He specified the type locality as the Cape of Good Hope. The specific epithet caffra is Modern Latin for South Africa. The Cape robin-chat was formerly assigned to the genus Cossypha but is now one of four species placed in the genus Dessonornis that was introduced in 1836 by Andrew Smith.


Subspecies
Four geographically isolated subspecies are generally accepted, though Clancey (1980, 1981) distinguished several additional races with contiguous ranges.

D. c. iolaemus (Reichenow, 1900) – south South Sudan to Malawi and north Mozambique
Widespread in Kenya, Tanzania and east Uganda. Easterly locations include Mount Marsabit, the Taita Hills, East Usambaras and the East African montane forests, southwards to Njombe, Songea, Mbeya and Ufipa. Westerly populations are present at Mount Elgon and nearby sites, besides the Marungu highlands in the eastern DRC
D. c. kivuensis (Schouteden, 1937) – southwest Uganda, northwest Tanzania and DRC, along rift
Ankole and Kigezi regions in south Uganda, and at Ngara, northwest Tanzania
D. c. namaquensis (W.L.Sclater, 1911) – Northern Cape and south Namibia
Especially in riparian vegetation of the lower Orange and Fish River regions
Somewhat larger than nominate, with broader white supercilium
D. c. caffer (Linnaeus, 1771) – east Zimbabwe, South Africa, Eswatini and Lesotho
Up to 2,200 m in Zimbabwe, where it does not breed below 1,400 m. South of the Limpopo however, it utilizes a greater range of habitats and altitudes, from sea level to 3,000 m, and is locally sympatric with red-capped and white-throated robin-chats.
Compared to D. c. iolaema it is slightly larger, less blackish on the head, with paler upper part plumage, less intense orange on breast, and greyer plumage below. Flanks still greyer in the far south, and chest plumage yellower.


Description

The sexes are similar. The Cape robin-chat measures 16–17 cm from bill tip to tail tip and weighs 28 g. The adult's upper parts are grey, with the mantle and secondary feathers tinged brownish olive. The blackish lores and ear coverts are separated from the crown by a prominent white supercilium. The chin, throat, central breast, rump, upper tail coverts and outer tail feathers are orange. The breast plumage moults to a deeper orange in colour for the non-breeding season. The central tail feathers are greyish-brown, and obscures the bright rufous outer tail feathers when the tail is closed. The belly is pale grey to white, and the undertail coverts buffy. The short, black bill is fairly straight, but with a slightly down-curved upper mandible. The legs and feet are black, and the eye is brown.
Juveniles have tails like the adults, but lack a supercilium. They are dark brown above and buff below, heavily marked with buff on the upper parts and grey-brown on the breast. The legs and feet are pinkish grey, and unlike other robin-chats, the soles of their feet are yellow. Immatures are like adults but retain some buff-tipped greater wing coverts.


Calls and song

The Cape robin-chat has a harsh, low, trisyllabic alarm call, which may be rendered as WA-dur-dra, WA-de-da or TURR-da-da. It has given rise to several local names, including "Jan frederik", which matches the rhythm of the call if the last syllables are run together. This call is also given year-round when arriving at, or departing from a roost.
The clear and ringing song is delivered in a series of short phrases, sometimes simultaneously by competing males. Singing peaks at dawn and dusk, and consists of passages of whistled syllables, cherooo-weet-weet-weeeet, followed by a pause. Each passage, consisting of 4 to 10 syllables, starts with a low, slurred whistle, which is followed by an improvised succession of whistled notes. From first light they usually sing from within, but near the top of a tree or bush, and at times uninterrupted for an hour or more. Singing may continue late into the evening, in pitch darkness. Males interrupt their singing during post-breeding moult. In the absence of her mate, females are said to sing equally well.
Anxious birds utter a plaintive, descending peeeeeuu note, for instance when their nestlings are threatened. Like other robin-chats, they may mimic other birds. One individual has been noted to mimic a total of 36 bird species.


Distribution and habitat
The Cape robin-chat occurs from South Sudan (mainly Imatong Mts, above 1,600 m) southwards to Uganda, the DRC (1,800 m – 3,475 m), Kenya, Tanzania, Zambia (above 1,800 m), Malawi (where common above 1,500 m), Zimbabwe, Namibia, South Africa, Eswatini and Lesotho. It is a mainly resident breeder in eastern and southern Africa, though some adults and juveniles may migrate more than 100 km to lower, warmer regions in winter. In their winter refuges, they may coexist with several other species of robin. Some are however year-round residents even at high altitudes. A five-year tenure of a breeding territory by the same pair is commonplace, and the maximum recorded life span is over 16 years.
In southern Africa, the Cape robin-chat is a common species at Afromontane forest edges, in forest scrub and ravines, fynbos, karoo, plantations, gardens and parks. Most areas with dense cover with scattered trees or song posts are however suitable. In dry areas they are restricted to thickets that fringe water courses. In southern Africa it is found from sea level up to 3,000 m where Leucosidea provides cover. It is absent from arid Karoo and Kalahari.
In tropical East Africa, the Cape robin-chat likewise frequents forest edge and gardens, but is restricted to the uplands. It occurs at 1,100 m in the Ulugurus, but generally upwards of 1,500 m in Uganda and the remainder of Tanzania, and above 1,800 m in Kenya. The tropical races are also at home at the edges of bamboo, in woodland, scrub, tea and coffee plantations, or in association with giant heather beside moorland streams, up to 3,400 m in altitude.


Behaviour
The Cape robin-chat moves about singly with a hopping gait, and often perches in prominent positions. It also roosts singly up to 3 metres aboves ground, in dense cover. The tail is regularly jerked up to an angle of 60 degrees, and upon alighting it may flick the wings and rapidly fan the tail. It bathes daily, even in tide pools.


Food and feeding
Generally, the Cape robin-chat forages close to or on ground level, but will on occasion glean bark and foliage in tall trees. It prefers the cover of vegetation, but is not very shy. Invertebrates, small frogs and lizards are obtained in scrub or on leaf litter. In addition fruit and seeds are plucked from plants or eaten on the ground. Occasionally an insect may be hawked in the air, or invertebrates may be gleaned from leaves, branches or rocks.


Breeding

The Cape robin-chats are monogamous and highly territorial nesters. A pair's territory usually comprises some fraction of a hectare, but its extent varies considerably depending on the habitat. The nest site is within 5 feet (regularly 0 – 2 feet) from the ground. It may be placed against a tree trunk, or on a broken stump in drift wood, and is often screened by overhanging vegetation.
The Cape robin-chat normally nests from June to November in the Western Cape and August to January elsewhere, but may nest at any time of the year. One of the pair will dowse its belly feathers and use the moisture to soften nesting material for easy shaping of the nest, while the other will bring the material to the nest. The female builds the cup-shaped nest of coarse vegetation, lined with animal hair, rootlets and other fine material. It is completed in 6 to 14 days, except when a nest is refurbished for a second clutch.
Two to three eggs are laid at one day intervals, and are incubated by the female for 14 to 19 days. The eggs measure 13 x 17 mm, and may be off-white, pinkish or pale blue, but always flecked with rusty brown, especially near the thicker end. Both parents will feed the nestlings during the subsequent 14 to 18 days, and for 5 to 7 weeks after they leave nest.
First breeding can occur at two years of age, but typically later, as potential territories are usually occupied. The Cape robin-chat is a host of the red-chested cuckoo. Predators often raid the nests, and replacement nests are common.


Gallery


References

Ian Sinclair, Phil Hockey and Warwick Tarboton, SASOL Birds of Southern Africa (Struik 2002) ISBN 1-86872-721-1


External links
Cape Robin Chat (nominate race), video of song and call vocalizations in western South Africa, Greg Morgan, YouTube
Cape Robin-Chat, sound recordings, xeno-canto
Cape (robin) chat - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds
Cape robin-chat Structured guide to the species in southern Africa

The '''Cape robin-chat''' ('''''Dessonornis caffer''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] of the [[Old World flycatcher]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Muscicapidae. It has a disjunct range from [[South Sudan]] to [[South Africa]].<ref name=geo>{{cite book |last=Chittenden |first=H. |title=Roberts geographic variation of southern African birds |year=2012 |publisher=JVBBF |location=Cape Town |isbn=978-1-920602-00-0 |pages=202–203 |display-authors=etal}}</ref>

The locally familiar and confiding [[species]]<ref name=gill>{{cite book|last1=Gill |first1=E. Leonard |last2=Winterbottom |first2=J.M. (revised) |title=A first guide to South African birds |date=1975 |publisher=Maskew Miller |location=Cape Town |isbn=0-623-00596-4 |pages=63–66 |edition=7th}}</ref> has colonized and benefited from a range of man-altered [[habitat]]s, including city suburbs and farmstead woodlots.<ref name="sabap1">{{cite web |last1=Oatley |first1=T. B. |title=SABAP1: Cape Robin |url=http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/601.pdf |website=sabap2.adu.org.za |publisher=SABAP |access-date=24 April 2016}}</ref> It is an accomplished songster like other robin-chats, but is rather less colourful than most, and frequents either drier settings or higher altitudes. It forages in the proximity of cover, in the open or in fairly well-lit environments. Its distribution resembles that of the [[Karoo thrush|karoo]]–[[Olive thrush|olive]] [[species complex|complex]] of thrushes, but it prefers the [[bracken]]-[[briar (thicket)|briar]] fringes of [[Afromontane forest]],<ref name="sabap1"/> and does not enter far into forest proper.<ref name=irw/>  It is altitudinally segregated from the [[red-capped robin-chat]],<ref name=irw>{{cite book |last1=Irwin |first1=Michael P. Stuart |title=The birds of Zimbabwe |date=1981 |publisher=Quest Pub. |location=Salisbury, Zimbabwe |isbn=0-86925-156-2 |pages=271–272}}</ref> and is less of a skulker.

==Taxonomy==
The Cape robin-chat was [[species description|formally described]] in 1771 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Motacilla caffra''. He specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as the [[Cape of Good Hope]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1771 | chapter=Regni Animalis Appendix. Aves | title=Mantissa plantarum altera. Generum editionis VI. & specierum editionis II | language=Latin | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | publisher=Salvius | page=527 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/51333885 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Paynter | editor2-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1964 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=10 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=57 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14486246 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''caffra'' is [[Neo-Latin|Modern Latin]] for South Africa.<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=83 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n83/mode/1up }}</ref> The Cape robin-chat was formerly assigned to the genus ''[[Cossypha]]'' but is now one of four species placed in the genus ''Dessonornis'' that was introduced in 1836 by [[Andrew Smith (zoologist)|Andrew Smith]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Chats, Old World flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/chats/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 July 2023}}</ref>

===Subspecies===
Four geographically isolated [[subspecies]] are generally accepted,<ref name=ioc/> though [[Phillip Clancey|Clancey]] (1980, 1981) distinguished several additional races with contiguous ranges.<ref name="sabap1"/>
* ''D. c. iolaemus'' ([[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow]], 1900) – south [[South Sudan]] to [[Malawi]] and north [[Mozambique]]
:: Widespread in Kenya, Tanzania and east Uganda.<ref name = brit/> Easterly locations include [[Mount Marsabit]], the [[Taita Hills]], [[Usambara Mountains|East Usambaras]] and the [[East African montane forests]], southwards to [[Njombe]], [[Songea]], [[Mbeya]] and Ufipa. Westerly populations are present at [[Mount Elgon]] and nearby sites,<ref name = brit/> besides the [[Marungu highlands]] in the eastern [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DRC]]<ref name="kei"/>
* ''D. c. kivuensis'' ([[Henri Schouteden|Schouteden]], 1937)<ref name="schout">{{cite journal |last1=Schouteden |first1=Henri |title=Quatre oiseaux du Kivu |journal=Revue de zoologie et de botanique africaines |date=1937 |volume=30 |pages=164–168 | issn = 0035-1814}}</ref> – southwest [[Uganda]], northwest [[Tanzania]] and [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DRC]], along rift
:: [[Ankole]] and [[Kigezi District|Kigezi]] regions in south Uganda, and at [[Ngara]], northwest Tanzania<ref name = brit/> 
* ''D. c. namaquensis'' ([[William Lutley Sclater|W.L.Sclater]], 1911) – [[Northern Cape]] and south [[Namibia]]
::Especially in riparian vegetation of the lower [[Orange River|Orange]] and [[Fish River (Namibia)|Fish River]] regions
::Somewhat larger than nominate,<ref name="kei"/> with broader white [[supercilium]]<ref name=geo/>
* ''D. c. caffer'' ([[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]], 1771) – east [[Zimbabwe]], [[South Africa]], [[Eswatini]] and [[Lesotho]]
::Up to 2,200 m in Zimbabwe, where it does not breed below 1,400 m.<ref name=irw/> South of the [[Limpopo River|Limpopo]] however, it utilizes a greater range of habitats and altitudes, from sea level to 3,000 m,<ref name="kei"/> and is locally sympatric with red-capped and [[white-throated robin-chat]]s.<ref name="sabap1"/>
::Compared to ''D. c. iolaema'' it is slightly larger,<ref name="kei"/> less blackish on the head, with paler upper part plumage, less intense orange on breast, and greyer plumage below. Flanks still greyer in the far south, and chest plumage yellower.<ref name=irw/>

==Description==
[[File:Cossypha caffra, Pretoria, c.jpg|thumb|right|The expanded tail reveals the greyish brown central, and rufous lateral rectrices.]]
The sexes are similar. The Cape robin-chat [[Bird measurement|measures]] 16–17 cm from bill tip to tail tip<ref name=zim>{{cite book |last=Zimmerman |first=Dale A. |title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania |year=1999 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0-691-01022-6 |page=495 |display-authors=etal}}</ref> and weighs 28 g.<ref name=rob7>{{cite book |last=Hockey |first=P.A.R. |title=Roberts Birds of Southern Africa |year=2005 |publisher=JVBBF |location=Cape Town |isbn=0-620-340-53-3 |pages=928–930 |display-authors=etal}}</ref> The adult's upper parts are grey, with the mantle and secondary feathers tinged brownish olive. The blackish lores and ear coverts are separated from the crown by a prominent white [[supercilium]].<ref name=rob7/> The chin, throat, central breast, rump, upper tail coverts and outer tail feathers are orange. The breast plumage moults to a deeper orange in colour for the non-breeding season.<ref name=geo/> The central tail feathers are greyish-brown, and obscures the bright rufous outer tail feathers when the tail is closed. The belly is pale grey to white, and the undertail coverts buffy.<ref name=rob7/> The short, black bill is fairly straight, but with a slightly down-curved upper mandible. The legs and feet are black, and the eye is brown.

Juveniles have tails like the adults, but lack a supercilium. They are dark brown above and buff below, heavily marked with buff on the upper parts and grey-brown on the breast. The legs and feet are pinkish grey, and unlike other robin-chats, the soles of their feet are yellow. Immatures are like adults but retain some buff-tipped greater wing coverts.<ref name=rob7/>

===Calls and song===
[[file:Cossypha caffra caffra, driegrepige keelklank, Pta, 2022-04-07 18h27, a.mp3 |thumb|right|Trisyllabic guttural calls when arriving at a roost at dusk]]
The Cape robin-chat has a harsh, low, trisyllabic alarm call, which may be rendered as ''{{not a typo|WA-dur-dra}}'', ''{{not a typo|WA-de-da}}''<ref name=gill/> or ''{{not a typo|TURR-da-da}}''.<ref name=zim/> It has given rise to several local names, including "Jan frederik", which matches the rhythm of the call if the last syllables are run together. This call is also given year-round when arriving at,<ref name=rob7/> or departing from a roost.

The clear and ringing song is delivered in a series of short phrases, sometimes simultaneously by competing males.<ref name=gill/> Singing peaks at dawn and dusk,<ref name=rob7/> and consists of passages of whistled syllables, ''{{not a typo|cherooo-weet-weet-weeeet}}'', followed by a pause. Each passage, consisting of 4 to 10 syllables, starts with a low, slurred whistle, which is followed by an improvised succession of whistled notes. From first light they usually sing from within, but near the top of a tree or bush, and at times uninterrupted for an hour or more.<ref name=ginn>{{cite book |last1=Ginn |first1=Peter |title=Birds of the highveld |date=1981 |publisher=Longman |location=Salisbury |isbn=0-582-60890-2 |page=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofhighveld0000ginn/page/112 112] |edition=3rd impr. |url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofhighveld0000ginn/page/112 }}</ref> Singing may continue late into the evening, in pitch darkness.<ref name=ginn/> Males interrupt their singing during post-breeding moult. In the absence of her mate, females are said to sing equally well.<ref name=rob7/>

Anxious birds utter a plaintive, descending ''{{not a typo|peeeeeuu}}'' note,<ref name=zim/> for instance when their nestlings are threatened.<ref name=rob7/> Like other robin-chats, they may mimic other birds. One individual has been noted to mimic a total of 36 bird species.<ref name=rob7/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The Cape robin-chat occurs from [[South Sudan]] (mainly [[Imatong Mountains|Imatong Mts]], above 1,600 m) southwards to [[Uganda]], the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo|DRC]] (1,800 m – 3,475 m), [[Kenya]], [[Tanzania]], [[Zambia]] (above 1,800 m), [[Malawi]] (where common above 1,500 m), [[Zimbabwe]], [[Namibia]], [[South Africa]], [[Eswatini]] and [[Lesotho]]. It is a mainly resident breeder in eastern and southern [[Africa]], though some adults and juveniles may migrate more than 100 km<ref name=rob7/> to lower, warmer regions in winter.<ref name=geo/> In their winter refuges, they may coexist with several other species of robin.<ref name="sabap1"/> Some are however year-round residents even at high altitudes.<ref name="sabap1"/> A five-year tenure of a breeding territory by the same pair is commonplace,<ref name=rob7/> and the [[maximum life span|maximum recorded life span]] is over 16 years.<ref name="paij">{{cite journal |last1=Paijmans |first1=Dane |title=Long-lived Cape Robin-Chat (Cossypha caffra) |journal=Bokmakierie |date=August 2016 |issue=246 |page=29 |url=http://witsbirdclub.org.za/wp-content/uploads/2016/09/Bokmakierie-246-August-2016.pdf |access-date=17 November 2016 |archive-date=18 November 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161118100741/http://witsbirdclub.org.za/wp-content/uploads/2016/09/Bokmakierie-246-August-2016.pdf }}</ref>

In southern Africa, the Cape robin-chat is a common species at [[Afromontane forest]] edges, in forest scrub and ravines, [[fynbos]], [[karoo]], plantations, gardens and parks. Most areas with dense cover with scattered trees or song posts are however suitable.<ref name="kei">{{cite book|last1=Keith|first1=Stuart|last2=Urban|first2=Emil K.|last3=Fry|first3=C. Hilary|title=The Birds of Africa, Volume IV|date=1992|publisher=Academic Press|isbn=978-0-12-137304-7|pages=423–425}}</ref> In dry areas they are restricted to thickets that fringe water courses.<ref name=wt/> In southern Africa it is found from sea level up to 3,000 m where ''[[Leucosidea]]'' provides cover.<ref name="sabap1"/> It is absent from arid [[Karoo]] and [[Kalahari]].

In tropical East Africa, the Cape robin-chat likewise frequents forest edge and gardens, but is restricted to the uplands.<ref name="brit">{{cite book|editor1-last=Britton |editor1-first=P.L.  |title=Birds of East Africa: 749. ''Cossypha caffra'' (L.) Robin Chat KTU 893 |date=1980 |publisher=EANHS |location=Nairobi |page=167}}</ref> It occurs at 1,100 m in the [[Uluguru Mountains|Ulugurus]], but generally upwards of 1,500 m in Uganda and the remainder of Tanzania, and above 1,800 m in Kenya. The tropical races are also at home at the edges of [[Yushania alpina|bamboo]], in woodland, scrub, tea and coffee plantations, or in association with [[Erica ventricosa|giant heather]] beside [[East African montane moorlands|moorland]] streams, up to 3,400 m in altitude.<ref name="kei"/><ref name="brit"/>

==Behaviour==
The Cape robin-chat moves about singly with a hopping gait, and often perches in prominent positions.<ref name=rob7/> It also roosts singly up to 3 metres aboves ground, in dense cover.<ref name=rob7/> The tail is regularly jerked up to an angle of 60 degrees, and upon alighting it may flick the wings and rapidly fan the tail. It bathes daily, even in [[tide pools]].<ref name=rob7/>

===Food and feeding===
Generally, the Cape robin-chat forages close to or on ground level, but will on occasion glean bark and foliage in tall trees.<ref name=zim/> It prefers the cover of vegetation, but is not very shy. [[Invertebrate]]s, small frogs and lizards are obtained in scrub or on leaf litter. In addition fruit and seeds are plucked from plants or eaten on the ground.<ref name=iziko>{{cite web|title=Cossypha caffra (Cape robin-chat, Cape robin) |url=http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/muscicapidae/cossypha_caffra.htm |website=biodiversity explorer |publisher=Iziko Museums |access-date=2 November 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304115005/http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/muscicapidae/cossypha_caffra.htm |archive-date=4 March 2016 }}</ref> Occasionally an insect may be hawked in the air, or invertebrates may be gleaned from leaves, branches or rocks.<ref name=iziko/>

===Breeding===
[[File:Piet-my-vrou & cape robin.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Cape robin-chat (at right) feeding a [[Cuculus solitarius|red-chested cuckoo]] parasite chick]]
The Cape robin-chats are monogamous and highly territorial nesters.<ref name=iziko/> A pair's territory usually comprises some fraction of a [[hectare]], but its extent varies considerably depending on the habitat.<ref name=wt>{{cite book|last=Tarboton |first=Warwick |title=A Guide to the Nests and Eggs of Southern African Birds |year=2001 |publisher=Struik |location=Cape Town |isbn=1-86872-616-9 |page=176}}</ref> The nest site is within 5 feet (regularly 0 – 2 feet) from the ground.<ref name=gill/><ref name=wt/> It may be placed against a tree trunk, or on a broken stump in drift wood, and is often screened by overhanging vegetation.<ref name=wt/>

The Cape robin-chat normally nests from June to November in the [[Western Cape]] and August to January elsewhere, but may nest at any time of the year. One of the pair will dowse its belly feathers and use the moisture to soften nesting material for easy shaping of the nest, while the other will bring the material to the nest. The female builds the cup-shaped nest of coarse vegetation, lined with animal hair, rootlets and other fine material. It is completed in 6 to 14 days, except when a nest is refurbished for a second clutch.<ref name=wt/>

Two to three eggs are laid at one day intervals,<ref name=wt/> and are incubated by the female for 14 to 19 days.<ref name=iziko/> The eggs measure 13 x 17 mm,<ref name=wt/> and may be off-white, pinkish or pale blue, but always flecked with rusty brown,<ref name=gill/> especially near the thicker end. Both parents will feed the nestlings during the subsequent 14 to 18 days, and for 5 to 7 weeks after they leave nest.<ref name=iziko/>

First breeding can occur at two years of age, but typically later, as potential territories are usually occupied.<ref name=rob7/> The Cape robin-chat is a host of the [[red-chested cuckoo]].<ref name=iziko/> Predators often raid the nests, and replacement nests are common.<ref name="sabap1"/>
